title: Auditing your household food waste
subtitle: Measuring what you throw away by category to find the 20 percent of changes that eliminate 80 percent of the waste
excerpt: The average UK household throws away 6.5 kg of food per week, worth around 60 pounds. WRAP data shows that fresh vegetables, bread, and ready meals account for over 60 percent of household food waste by weight. A two-week audit by category tells you where your waste actually comes from, which is rarely where you assume it does.
sourceNotes: WRAP Household Food Waste UK 2018 report. WRAP Love Food Hate Waste measurement methodology. DEFRA UK household food waste statistics 2021.

glossaryTerms.definition: An economic model that prioritises keeping products and materials in use for as long as possible through reuse, repair, and sharing, rather than a linear take-make-dispose pattern.

content.text: WRAP estimates UK households waste 6.6 million tonnes of food per year. Around 70 percent of that (by weight) is classified as avoidable: food that was edible at some point before disposal. The 30 percent that is unavoidable (peelings, bones, teabags) can be composted. A household audit answers a specific question: what proportion of your food waste is avoidable, and which product categories drive it?
For two weeks, record everything before it goes in the bin or compost. Note the item, the approximate quantity, and the reason it was thrown away.
At the end of two weeks, group entries by category. Key categories are fresh vegetables, bread, dairy, meat, cooked meals, fruit, and ambient products such as tins and dry goods.

Identify the top two or three categories by volume. WRAP's national data shows these are almost always fresh vegetables (salad, herbs), bread, and cooked meals. If your audit shows the same, those are the categories to address.

Fresh vegetable waste is predominantly caused by over-purchasing and incorrect storage. Root vegetables (carrots, parsnips, celeriac) last 2 to 3 weeks in a cool, dark store or the salad drawer; most people keep them at room temperature and throw them away in 5 to 7 days. Fresh herbs are the single highest-waste item by value per kilogram: a 29-pence packet of supermarket parsley used for one recipe and discarded represents a 90 percent waste rate. Buy growing herbs in pots and keep them on the windowsill; they last 4 to 8 weeks with watering.

Bread stays fresh for 3 to 5 days at room temperature. In a household that does not eat a full loaf in that time, freeze the second half on day one (not day five when it is already stale). Frozen bread defrosts in 2 to 3 hours at room temperature or 45 seconds in a toaster from frozen. A 750 g loaf that is half-wasted costs around 75 pence in waste food; at 5 loaves per year, the saving is small but the habit generalises to other foods.
What to track after the audit

The audit is a one-time diagnostic. After identifying the top categories, make two or three targeted changes (weekly meal plan, adjusted shop quantities, different storage location for one product category) and run a one-week re-check in a month. The goal is to reduce avoidable waste by half: from a national average of around 400 grams per person per day to 200 grams. At 60 pounds per household per week for food shopping, a 50 percent reduction in avoidable waste saves around 20 to 30 pounds per month. This is a
gain that requires no new equipment and no capital cost.

attrs.symptom: Audit log abandoned after three days

content.text: Simplify: use five categories only (veg, fruit, bread, dairy, other) and just tick a box when something goes in the bin, without recording the reason. Even a tally count by category over two weeks gives enough data to identify the top problem area.

attrs.symptom: Waste seems equally spread across all categories

content.text: This sometimes indicates a planning gap: buying food without a clear plan for how it will be used, so random items across all categories spoil. A weekly meal plan for 3 to 4 dinners and lunches (not all meals, just the planned ones) usually reveals a consistent category gap and reduces across-the-board spoilage.
